title: SEGMENT 12 — Chimpanzee adenovirus vectors: ChAdOx1 and ChAd3 sidestepping pre-existing immunity

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Chimpanzee adenovirus vectors: ChAdOx1 and ChAd3 sidestepping pre-existing immunity
The idea of using non-human adenoviruses as vectors goes back to the early 2000s, when the seroprevalence problem with Ad5 was becoming clear.
Researchers reasoned: if the problem is that humans have been exposed to human adenoviruses, then we should use an adenovirus from a species humans don't share viruses with.
Chimpanzees were the obvious candidate.

Chimpanzees, our closest evolutionary relatives, carry adenoviruses with overall structures very similar to human adenoviruses — so the molecular biology of vector construction would be familiar — but with surface antigens different enough that human immune systems have not seen them before.
The best-known chimpanzee adenoviral vector is ChAdOx1 — chimpanzee adenovirus Oxford 1 — developed at the Jenner Institute at Oxford in the 2010s.
It is based on a chimpanzee adenovirus called Y25, with the E1 region deleted and replaced with an expression cassette for whatever antigen you want to encode.

The vector was originally developed for influenza and tuberculosis vaccines, and then in early 2020, when SARS-CoV-2 emerged, the Oxford team rapidly cloned the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein gene into ChAdOx1 and produced what became the AstraZeneca-Oxford COVID-19 vaccine, also called Vaxzevria or Covishield.
There is a related vector called ChAd3, based on a different chimpanzee adenovirus, that was developed earlier and was used in Ebola vaccines.
The analogy I want to use here.
Switching from Ad5 to ChAdOx1 is switching from a delivery company everyone in town has dealt with before — and remembers — to a brand-new delivery company that no one has seen.
When the new company's truck pulls up, nobody recognizes the logo.
No neighborhood watch is on alert.
No barricades go up.
The truck delivers its package without incident.
That novelty is the entire selling point.
The pre-existing immunity to ChAdOx1 in human populations is essentially zero — typically less than 5 percent, often less than 1 percent.
So you can dose almost anyone and expect the vector to evade neutralization.
The vector reaches target cells, the encoded antigen is expressed in those cells, the antigen is presented to the immune system, and a robust adaptive response develops against the encoded antigen.
Which is exactly what you want from a vaccine.
There is, of course, an asterisk.
After the first dose, the patient does develop anti-ChAdOx1 antibodies.
So a second dose of ChAdOx1 — a homologous boost — is partially neutralized by the antibodies the patient has already generated against the first dose.
The AstraZeneca vaccine showed reduced efficiency on boost compared to prime, and considerable optimization went into the dosing interval to maximize the overall response.

Heterologous boosting — for example, ChAdOx1 prime followed by an mRNA vaccine boost — was widely studied during the pandemic and turned out to produce excellent immune responses, often better than two doses of either vaccine alone.

The construction of ChAdOx1 follows the same first-generation logic as Ad5-based vectors: delete E1, delete most of E3, leave E2 and E4 intact, and insert your transgene expression cassette in place of the deleted E1.

The vector is produced in HEK293 cells or T-REx-293 cells modified to express the chimpanzee adenoviral E1 proteins (which differ slightly from the Ad5 E1 proteins that HEK293 endogenously expresses).

The producer cells supply E1 in trans, the vector replicates and packages, and you harvest by the same purification protocols used for Ad5 — cesium chloride centrifugation or anion exchange chromatography (Segment 17).

One nice property of ChAdOx1 is that, like Ad5, it is non-replicating in patient cells, it doesn't integrate, and expression is transient — peaking at a few days post-administration and declining over a few weeks.
For a vaccine, transient expression is fine; you just need long enough for the antigen to be presented and the adaptive immune response to be primed.
For a gene therapy, transient expression is a limitation, but vaccines are where ChAdOx1 has had its most dramatic impact.
Now I want to flag two safety issues that emerged specifically with adenoviral COVID-19 vaccines, because the story has to be told honestly.

Both ChAdOx1-based vaccines (AstraZeneca) and Ad26-based vaccines (Johnson and Johnson) were associated with a rare but serious side effect called vaccine-induced immune thrombotic thrombocytopenia, or VITT.

VITT involves the formation of antibodies against a platelet protein called PF4, which can drive abnormal blood clotting in unusual locations — cerebral venous sinuses, splanchnic veins — sometimes with fatal consequences.
The mechanism is not fully understood, but it appears to involve a complex interaction between the adenoviral capsid, plasma proteins including PF4, and the patient's immune system.

VITT is rare — incidence around one in 100,000 doses or less, depending on the population — but it was severe enough to lead to changes in vaccination guidance in many countries, particularly for younger patients with available alternative vaccines.
This is a sobering footnote.
Adenoviral vaccines saved a great many lives during the COVID-19 pandemic — particularly in low and middle income countries where AstraZeneca was the most widely deployed vaccine.
But they also carried a small but real risk of severe side effects that we did not fully appreciate before deployment.
The field is still working to understand VITT mechanistically, and that understanding will inform the next generation of adenoviral vaccines.
So that's chimpanzee adenoviral vectors.
Engineered to dodge the pre-existing immunity problem.
Powerful as vaccine vehicles.
With known limitations and a small set of rare but serious adverse events.
The next segment will put this in the broader context of how the COVID era reshaped adenoviral vector deployment globally.
